content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 12, 2019

Mark Ross
Ross Company, Realtors
RCR Property Management

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-183

Dear Mr. Ross:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Under the Act, may you take part in the Martinez City Council's decisions on whether to grant a conditional use permit and amend the Central Commercial zoning plan, such that a sporting goods store may be permitted to operate a shooting range in its basement, given the various economic interests detailed in your request for advice?

CONCLUSION

Based on the financial interests identified, you may take part in the permit and zoning decisions. The possibility of a sporting goods store operating a shooting range would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on any of the economic interests detailed in your request for advice. With respect to your family's businesses—a source of income for you—the decisions would not have a foreseeable impact on annual gross revenues or expenses, nor is there clear and convincing evidence of a substantial effect on any of the businesses' real property interests. Additionally, two of your real property interests are far enough away from the area

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-183
Page No. 2

affected by the decision, such that the decisions would presumably have no material effect (and
there is no clear and convincing evidence rebutting that presumption). In regard to your rental
property between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Central Commercial zoning area that would be
affected by the amendment, it is not reasonably foreseeable the decision would have a material
financial effect on your interests in the property or rental business. However, if there are additional
facts indicating that the decision may affect a tenant's income, investments, or other assets or
liabilities or implicate a source of commission income other than Ross Company Realtors and RCR
Property Management, you may be prohibited from participating in the decision.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are a member of the Martinez ("City") City Council ("City Council"). The City Council
is currently considering the granting of a Conditional Use Permit and an amendment to the Central
Commercial zoning for a building located in the Downtown area of Martinez, at 600 Ferry Street. It
is currently at the planning commission level, and has not yet been granted or denied. During a
follow-up phone call, you clarified that the permit and amendment decisions would allow (or
prohibit) a sporting goods store to operate a gun range in the store's basement as a recreational
facility. The decision will likely reach the City Council in September.

You also work as an independent contractor real estate broker for Ross Company Realtors,
and as an employee property manager for RCR Property Management. RCR Property Management
is a family business and S corporation. You are a vice president in the company's corporation
structure, but have no ownership or equity interest. You have never had an ownership or equity
interest, nor has any ownership or equity interest been promised. The business is entirely owned by
your father, Gene Ross. The business premises are on a long term (five year plus) lease, with the
rent prescribed, and is located at 928 Main Street, in Downtown Martinez.

You receive compensation as a real estate broker. You receive commission from sales, with
a small split going to Ross Company Realtors. You currently have one property listing in the
downtown area on Estudillo Street, a church building for sale. You have no offers on the property at
this time. Your father has a Ferry Street property listing for sale approximately one block away
from the subject site, and you have helped him market it, but you have not received any promise of
compensation, nor do you have any expectation of it. Your father manages the property as part of
his $300/month compensation from RCR Property Management. Your duties as an employee of
RCR Property Management do not involve the Ferry Street property and your compensation does
not come directly from this property, although you may change a light bulb or smoke detector
battery for him once a year.

As a property manager for RCR Property Management, you derive income based on the
number of properties you manage, and the leasing commissions you get from leasing residences
(you have several small commercial accounts but not in Martinez City limits). You manage two
properties in the Downtown Martinez area: a single-family dwelling at 615 Green Street and a four-
plex at 1000 Las Juntas Street. The compensation is $125 and $250 respectively, and goes to RCR
Property Management directly, then to you as salary. In July, you received a $558 commission in
July for a leasing fee for an apartment at 1000 Las Juntas.

File No. A-19-183
Page No. 3

You own a personal residence on Andrews Drive, well away from the Downtown core. You
also own two rental properties—one located on Pacheco Boulevard and the other on Pine Street.
The Pacheco Boulevard property is also located well away from the Downtown core, and the Pine
Street property is located "approximately a little more [than] 1,000 feet" from the subject building
and "approximately a little more than 500 feet from the closest edge of the Central Commercial
district for which the amendment is being sought."

ANALYSIS

Economic Interests

Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest." Section 87103 further specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on certain enumerated economic interests. Those
economic interests include:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more;
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more;
• Any source of income . . . aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made; and
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

(Section 87103.)

Source of Income Interests

You receive a base salary for your work as a real estate broker and property manager for
your family's business. Because your earnings are greater than $500 in the past twelve months, you
have a source of income interest in Ross Company Realtors and RCR Property Management.
(Section 87103(c).) Additionally, you have noted that you receive income based on the number of
properties you manage, as well as commission income from leasing residences, including a $558
commission in July for a leasing fee for an apartment at the Las Juntas property.

Regulation 18700.1 provides various provisions relating to commission income for real
estate brokers and agents. Under Regulation 18700.1(c)(2)(B), a real estate broker has a source of
income interest in all of the following sources of commission income if the commission income
aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision: (1) the person the
broker represents in the transaction; (2) the person represented by a agent working under the
broker's auspices if the broker receives commission from the transaction; (3) any broker business

File No. A-19-183
Page No. 4

entity through which the broker conducts business; and (4) any person who receives a finder's or
other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker.

You also have source of income interests in any tenant of your rental properties who has
paid you at least $500 in the twelve months prior to the applicable governmental decision. (See
Section 87103(c).)²

Business Entity Interests

Although you have no ownership or equity interest in your family's business, you are a vice
president and, thus, an officer in the organization's corporate structure. Accordingly, you have an
economic interest in your family's business under the Act. (Section 87103(d).) You also have
business entity interests in your rental properties, as you presumably have a direct or indirect
investment of at least $2,000 in each of the properties. (Section 87103(a).)

Real Property Interests

Your personal property on Andrews Drive, as well as your rental properties on Pacheco
Boulevard and Pine Street, presumably involve direct or indirect investments of $2,000 or more,
and thus constitute real property economic interests. You do not have a direct or indirect³
investment of $2,000 or more in:

• The Main Street property where your family's business is located;
• Your church building property listing on Estudillo Street;
• Your father's property listing on Ferry Street;
• The property you manage on Green Street; or
• The property you manage on Las Juntas Street.

Thus, the above five properties do not constitute real property economic interests under the Act.
(Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

Under Regulation 18701(a), a financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or subject of, the governmental
decision. Based upon the fact provided, none of the interests identified are explicitly involved in the

² We do not analyze the effect of the applicable governmental decisions on any source of income interest that
you may have in a tenant in your residential rental properties or source of commission income other than Ross
Company Realtors and RCR Property Management, because you have not provided any facts regarding any particular
tenant or source of commission income. We note, however, that you should seek further advice if there is any indication
that any such decision may affect a tenant's income, investments, or other assets or liabilities or implicate a source of
commission income other than Ross Company Realtors and RCR Property Management.
³ For purposes of determining an official's economic interests under the Act, an "indirect investment or
interest" means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 87103.)

File No. A-19-183
Page No. 5

decision. Accordingly, Regulation 18701(b), which sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable
to an official's economic interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, applies and provides
that "[a] financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable." (Regulation 18701(b).)

Sources of Income and Business Entities

Under Regulation 18702.3(a)(4), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on an official's financial interest in a source of income is material if the
source of income is a business entity that will be financially affected under the materiality standards
in Regulation 18702.1. Regulation 18702.1, in turn, provides that where a public official's business
entity interest is not explicitly involved in the relevant governmental decision, the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the decision on the business entity is material if:

• The decision may result in an increase or decrease of the entity's annual gross revenues, or
the value of the entity's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000,
or five percent of the entity's annual gross revenues and the increase or decrease is at least
$10,000; or

• The decision may cause the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate expenses in an amount equal to or more than $250,000, or one percent of the
entity's annual gross revenues and the change in expenses is at least $2,500.

(Regulation 18702.1(a)(2)-(3).) Additionally, the financial effect of a governmental decision on an
official's business entity interest is considered material if the official knows or has reason to know
that the entity has an interest in real property and there is clear and convincing evidence that the
decision would have a substantial effect on the property. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(4)(B).)

As noted above, you have a source of income interest and business entity interest in Ross
Company Realtors and RCR Property Management. You also have business entity interests in your
own rental properties. Those interests are analyzed under the same materiality standards. Based on
the facts provided, it does not appear that the decisions of whether to grant a conditional use permit
and amend the central commercial zoning—such that a sporting goods store would be permitted to
sell firearms and operate a shooting range—would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial
effect on any of those interests. The decisions would not foreseeably cause any of the business
entities to incur, avoid, reduce, or eliminate any expenses, nor is there any indication that the
decisions would result in an increase or decrease of at least $10,000 in annual gross revenues.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(2)-(3).) Lastly, there is no clear and convincing evidence that either
decision would have a substantial effect on any of the business entities' real property interests.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)

Real Property Interests

"The financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an
official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more from the property line of the

File No. A-19-183
Page No. 6

official's property is presumed not to be material." (Regulation 18702.2(b).) However, "[t]his
presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the governmental decision would
have a substantial effect on the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(b).)

As noted above, you have real property interests in your personal residence and a rental
property on Pacheco Boulevard. You note that both properties are located "well away" from the
"Downtown core" of Martinez. Provided that both properties are located 1,000 feet or more from
the real property affected by the governmental decisions before—i.e., 600 Ferry Street and the
surrounding Central Commercial zone—any financial effect on those real property interests is
presumably not material, and there is no clear and convincing evidence to rebut that presumption.

You also have a rental property located on Pine Street, located "approximately a little more
[than] 1,000 feet from the subject building," and "approximately a little more than 500 feet from the
closest edge of the Central Commercial district for which the amendment is being sought." Under
Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on
a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than a leasehold interest,
is material whenever the governmental decision involves property located more than 500 feet but
less than 1,000 feet from the property line of the parcel, and the decision would change the parcel's
development potential, income producing potential, highest and best use, character (by substantially
altering traffic levels, intensity of use, parking, view, privacy, noise levels, or quality of use), or market
value. There is nothing to indicate that the amendment to the Central Commercial zoning would
have any such effect on your rental property.

Based on the foregoing, we conclude that you do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the Act, and you may take part in the decisions regarding the potential conditional use permit
and zoning amendment.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

B. Natt 8/10

By:     Kevin Cornwall
         Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:sal

grant a conditional use permit and amend the Central Commercial zoning plan, such that a sporting 
goods store may be permitted to operate a shooting range in its basement, given the various 
economic interests detailed in your request for advice?

interests. Additionally, two of your real property interests are far enough away from the areaaffected by the decision, such that the decisions would presumably have no material effect (and

Property Management, you may be prohibited from participating in the decision.

sections.analysis:
Economic Interests 
Section 87100 of the Act provides, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest." Section 87103 further specifies that a public official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a 
member of his or her immediate family, or on certain enumerated economic interests. Those 
economic interests include: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more; 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more; 
• 
Any source of income ... aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value 
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made; and 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103.) 
Source of Income Interests 
You receive a base salary for your work as a real estate broker and property manager for 
your family's business. Because your earnings are greater than $500 in the past twelve months, you 
have a source of income interest in Ross Company Realtors and RCR Property Management. 
(Section 87103(c).) Additionally, you have noted that you receive income based on the number of 
properties you manage, as well as commission income from leasing residences, including a $558 
commission in July for a leasing fee for an apartment at the Las Juntas property. 
Regulation 18700.1 provides various provisions relating to commission income for real 
estate brokers and agents. Under Regulation 18700.l(c)(2)(B), a real estate broker has a source of 
income interest in all of the following sources of commission income if the commission income 
aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision: (1) the person the 
broker represents in the transaction; (2) the person represented by a agent working under the 
broker's auspices if the broker receives commission from the transaction; (3) any broker business  
entity through which the broker conducts business; and (4) any person who receives a finder's or 
other referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the broker; or who makes a referral 
pursuant to a contract with the broker. 
You also have source of income interests in any tenant of your rental properties who has 
paid you at least $500 in the twelve months prior to the applicable governmental decision, (See

Although you have no ownership or equity interest in your family's business, you are a vice

business entity interests in your rental properties, as you presumably have a direct or indirect 
investment of at least $2,000 in each of the properties. (Section 87103(a).) 
Real Property Interests 
Your personal property on Andrews Drive, as well as your rental properties on Pacheco 
Boulevard and Pine Street, presumably involve direct or indirect investments of $2,000 or more, 
and thus constitute real property economic interests. You do not have a direct or indirect3 
investment of $2,000 or more in: 
• 
The Main Street property where your family's business is located; 
• 
Your church building property listing on Estudillo Street; 
• 
Your father's property listing on Ferry Street; 
• 
The property you manage on Green Street; or 
• 
The property you manage on Las Juntas Street. 
Thus, the above five properties do not constitute real property economic interests under the Act. 
(Section 87103(b).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under Regulation 1870 I ( a), a financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or subject of, the governmental 
decision. Based upon the fact provided, none of the interests identified are explicitly involved in the

behalfof a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and

decision. Accordingly, Regulation 1870l(b), which sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable

the value of the entity's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000,

decision would have a substantial effect on any of the business entities' real property interests.

official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more from the property line of the

official's property is presumed not to be material." (Regulation 18702.2(b).) However, "[t]his 
presumption may be rebutted with clear and convincing evidence the governmental decision would 
have a substantial effect on the official's property." (Regulation 18702.2(b).) 
As noted above, you have real property interests in your personal residence and a rental

grant a conditional use permit and amend the Central Commercial zoning plan, such that a sporting 
goods store may be permitted to operate a shooting range in its basement, given the various 
economic interests detailed in your request for advice?

CONCLUSION: Based on the financial interests identified, you may take part in the permit and zoning 
decisions. The possibility of a sporting goods store operating a shooting range would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on any of the economic interests detailed in your 
request for advice. With respect to your family's businesses-a source of income for you-the 
decisions would not have a foreseeable impact on annual gross revenues or expenses, nor is there 
clear and convincing evidence of a substantial effect on any of the businesses' real property 
interests. Additionally, two of your real property interests are far enough away from the areaaffected by the decision, such that the decisions would presumably have no material effect (and 
there is no clear and convincing evidence rebutting that presumption). In regard to your rental 
property between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Central Commercial zoning area that would be 
affected by the amendment, it is not reasonably foreseeable the decision would have a material 
financial effect on your interests in the property or rental business. However, if there are additional 
facts indicating that the decision may affect a tenant's income, investments, or other assets or

embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 12, 2019 Mark Ross Ross Company, Realtors RCR Property Management Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-183 Dear Mr. Ross: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION Under the Act, may you take part in the Martinez City Council's decisions on whether to grant a conditional use permit and amend the Central Commercial zoning plan, such that a sporting goods store may be permitted to operate a shooting range in its basement, given the various economic interests detailed in your request for advice? CONCLUSION Based on the financial interests identified, you may take part in the permit and zoning decisions. The possibility of a sporting goods store operating a shooting range would not have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on any of the economic interests detailed in your request for advice. With respect to your family's businesses—a source of income for you—the decisions would not have a foreseeable impact on annual gross revenues or expenses, nor is there clear and convincing evidence of a substantial effect on any of the businesses' real property interests. Additionally, two of your real property interests are far enough away from the area ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-183 Page No. 2 affected by the decision, such that the decisions would presumably have no material effect (and there is no clear and convincing evidence rebutting that presumption). In regard to your rental property between 500 and 1,000 feet from the Central Commercial zoning area that would be affected by the amendment, it is not reasonably foreseeable the decision would have a material financial effect on your interests in the property or rental business. However, if there are additional facts indicating that the decision may affect a tenant's income, investments, or other assets or liabilities or implicate a source of commission income other than Ross Company Realtors and RCR
